column1: # Source: Z-Beam Laser Cleaning Research Lab
# Citation: Z-Beam (2025). Silicon Laser Cleaning Dataset. Retrieved from https://www.z-beam.com/materials/semiconductor/elemental/silicon-laser-cleaning

column9: Description

Optimal average power for Silicon surface cleaning without substrate damage
Near-IR wavelength with good Silicon absorption for contaminant removal
Beam spot diameter for precise cleaning with adequate energy density
Optimal repetition rate for efficient cleaning coverage and process control
Fluence threshold for effective contaminant removal without Silicon damage
Nanosecond pulse duration for effective contaminant ablation with minimal thermal diffusion
Optimal scanning speed for uniform cleaning coverage
Number of cleaning passes for complete contaminant removal
Optimal beam overlap for uniform cleaning without excessive thermal buildup

Typical range for industrial laser cleaning of semiconductors like Silicon to balance efficiency and avoid thermal damage; starting at 50 W for light contamination. Max safe below ~200 W to prevent melting (Silicon melting point 1414°C). Source: IPG Photonics YLR series specs for cleaning applications; academic review in Optics and Lasers in Engineering (2018).

Effective range for 1064 nm cleaning; 1000 mm/s optimal for uniform contaminant removal on Silicon without excessive heat buildup due to its high thermal conductivity (~150 W/m·K). Light contamination: 2000-5000 mm/s; heavy: 100-500 mm/s. Source: Industry standards from Laserline GmbH manuals; case studies in Journal of Applied Physics (2020).

Nanosecond regime (5-200 ns) common for industrial 1064 nm cleaning of Silicon; 20 ns recommended for minimal heat-affected zone (HAZ <10 μm) by confining energy to surface ablation. Picosecond (ps) or femtosecond (fs) better for ultra-precision but costlier; ns sufficient for contamination removal. Source: Laser physics research in Applied Surface Science (2019); avoids reflectivity issues at 1064 nm (~35% for Silicon).

Typical 10-100 kHz for pulsed cleaning; 50 kHz recommended for Silicon to achieve fast coverage while limiting heat accumulation (trade-off: higher rates speed up process but risk thermal stress). Lower rates for heavy contamination. Source: Academic literature in Optics Express (2021); based on Silicon's moderate IR absorption.

Fluence range for safe cleaning below Silicon's ablation threshold (~1-3 J/cm² at 1064 nm); 0.5 J/cm² recommended to remove contaminants without substrate damage. Damage threshold ~2 J/cm² for ns pulses. Conservative for Silicon's reflectivity. Source: Ablation threshold studies in Physical Review B (2017); Journal of Laser Applications (2022).

Typical beam diameter for cleaning; 200 μm recommended for Silicon surface preparation, balancing resolution for fine features and coverage for larger areas. Smaller (50-100 μm) for detailed work; larger (300-500 μm) for broad contamination. Source: Equipment specs from Coherent ArmoClean systems; industry optimization in Surface and Coatings Technology (2020).

1-5 passes typical for complete removal; 2 passes recommended for Silicon to ensure thorough cleaning of industrial contaminants without overexposure. Single pass for light layers; multi-pass for heavy or uneven. Source: Industry practice in semiconductor processing case studies, e.g., SPIE Proceedings (2019).

30-80% overlap for uniform coverage; 60% recommended for Silicon cleaning to avoid gaps or streaks while minimizing total energy input and heat. Higher (70-80%) for fine uniformity; lower (30-50%) for speed on large areas. Source: Process optimization studies in Lasers in Manufacturing and Materials Processing (2021).
